channel.title: Cloud Computing on Lucky Taorem | Tech & AI Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Cloud Computing on Lucky Taorem | Tech & AI Blog

item.title: Google Buys Solar Farm Output
item.description:
Introduction to Google’s Sustainability Efforts  

Google has recently struck a deal to purchase the entire electrical output from the Steel River Energy Center solar project in Arkansas. This move is aimed at offsetting the company’s fossil fuel emissions, which have been increasing due to the growing demand for its data centers. The Steel River Energy Center solar project is expected to come online in 2029 and will use US-manufactured steel and solar panels.

item.title: New York Bans Data Center Construction
item.description:
Introduction to Data Center Construction Ban  

New York has become the first state in the US to enact a ban on data center construction, aiming to promote environmental sustainability. This move is expected to have significant implications for the tech industry, particularly for companies reliant on large data centers. Governor Kathy Hochul signed an executive order pausing environmental permits for large data centers consuming more than 50 megawatts for up to a year. As reported, “New York will lead the way in creating the strongest standards in the nation for data center development, ensuring that when companies succeed because of New York, New Yorkers succeed too.”

item.title: Kyber: The Secret Tech Powering Millions of Future Robots
item.description:
The Man Behind VLC Is Now Building the Backbone of Physical AI  

Jean-Baptiste Kempf is a name that may not ring a bell for the average internet user, but his work has touched nearly every person on the planet. As the lead developer of VLC Media Player, Kempf helped create one of the most ubiquitous pieces of open-source software in history, with over 6 billion downloads to date. Now, he’s turning his attention to a challenge that could be even more transformative: building the infrastructure that will power millions of robots and drones in the real world.
